name: LiteLLM
title: LiteLLM integration: end-to-end replay on cheaper models
description: rightmodeler re-runs your real pipeline with a LiteLLM proxy injecting the cheaper candidate, tools fire, loops run, and the report shows whether the swap survived.
lede: When a step calls tools or loops, judging one replayed completion is not proof. rightmodeler re-runs your real pipeline with a LiteLLM proxy swapping the model underneath, your code unmodified, the candidate doing the actual work.
tldr: A LiteLLM proxy can be rightmodeler's replay provider: set RIGHTMODELER_PROVIDER=litellm and candidate and judge calls use the proxy's live catalog, while x-litellm-response-cost supplies each call's charge when present. LiteLLM also remains the documented injection method for end-to-end replay, where you set litellm_params.api_base to the active replay provider and your pipeline re-runs unmodified in a throwaway git worktree.

steps.label: 01 · Configure
steps.title: One config file maps the model underneath
steps.body: A LiteLLM config's model_list maps the model name your code requests to the candidate under test. Set litellm_params.model to that provider's model id, litellm_params.api_base to the active replay provider's base URL, and api_key to the matching provider key. When LiteLLM itself is selected with RIGHTMODELER_PROVIDER=litellm, rightmodeler uses the aliases exposed by the proxy's live catalog.

steps.label: 02 · Redirect
steps.title: Point the pipeline at localhost, not the provider
steps.body: For proxy injection, litellm --config config.yaml starts the gateway and OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000 points your unmodified code at it; the pipeline keeps requesting the model it always requests. When LiteLLM is the active replay provider, the sandbox instead sets OPENAI_BASE_URL to LITELLM_PROXY_API_BASE and also sets the native Anthropic SDK base URL before the pipeline starts.

steps.label: 03 · Re-run
steps.title: The real pipeline, in a throwaway worktree
steps.body: rightmodeler re-runs your pipeline from a detached git worktree of your repo's HEAD, seeded with the recorded task input; the candidate takes its own trajectory rather than replaying your old turns. Tools actually fire: side-effecting ones are mocked from the trace, read-only ones run live, and non-model HTTP is frozen with vcrpy.

steps.label: 04 · Compare
steps.title: Three axes, then a verdict
steps.body: The original and candidate runs are compared on tool-call sequence, step and loop count with the cost delta, and final output: deterministic trajectory matching for tool-selection regressions, a trajectory judge for valid-but-different paths. The verdict lands in the per-step report, and you approve, reject, or hold each swap in the TUI.

setup.intro: There is nothing to install into your app. LiteLLM can be the selected replay provider through an existing proxy, or it can run only inside the replay sandbox as an injection layer in front of your unmodified pipeline.

commands.comment: # make your proxy the replay provider itself
# what the E2E replay stands up in front of your code
# inside Claude Code, hand the skill your traces and the repo to re-run
commands.command: claude "run the rightmodeler skill on ./traces and confirm swaps against this repo"

useCases.title: Prove a swap survives tool calls and loops
useCases.body: A step that chooses tools cannot be judged from one replayed completion. The end-to-end re-run shows whether the candidate picks the same calls with the same arguments, stays out of extra loop iterations, and still lands the final output.

useCases.title: Audit an Anthropic-SDK pipeline
useCases.body: When LiteLLM is the selected replay provider, rightmodeler points the native Anthropic SDK at the configured proxy before your pipeline starts, so your code joins the replay without an edit.

useCases.title: Reuse the gateway you already run
useCases.body: If LiteLLM already fronts your LLM traffic, set RIGHTMODELER_PROVIDER=litellm and rightmodeler uses that proxy's catalog for replay. Each response's x-litellm-response-cost header supplies the candidate's per-call charge when the proxy returns it.

useCases.title: Catch cascades before they ship
useCases.body: A cheaper model that drifts on one step can wreck the steps that consume its output. Re-running the whole pipeline surfaces those failures while they are still a report finding, not an incident.

limits: rightmodeler natively ingests LiteLLM proxy StandardLoggingPayload logs through a dedicated adapter. LiteLLM can be both a trace source and the selected replay provider.

Replays are not bit-exact even at temperature 0 with a fixed seed; GPU float non-associativity and MoE routing still drift. Important steps are re-run several times and read as a distribution, not a single sample.
When a mocked side-effecting tool is called with different arguments than the trace recorded, there is no stored answer; that replay mainly measures whether the candidate picks the same calls.

High-risk step families (auth, payments, migrations, deploys, secrets) are flagged and left on the current model, and weak evidence means abstain. A tool that always finds a swap is not measuring anything.
The proxy lives and dies inside the replay sandbox. rightmodeler never routes production traffic through LiteLLM or anything else; it is an offline report, not a hop in your request path.

faq.q: Do I need to run LiteLLM to use rightmodeler?
faq.a: No, unless you choose it as the replay provider. With RIGHTMODELER_PROVIDER=litellm, single-shot replays and judge calls go through the configured proxy and per-call charges come from x-litellm-response-cost when present. With another provider selected, LiteLLM enters only when an end-to-end replay uses it as the local injection layer.

No, they do different jobs. LiteLLM routes live traffic; rightmodeler is an offline audit that borrows it as a swap point during replay and never sits in your request path. If a LiteLLM gateway already fronts your traffic, keep it. Crucible, the planned optimization suite, names LiteLLM as a bring-your-own-routing option.

faq.q: Can rightmodeler ingest my LiteLLM proxy logs?
faq.a: Yes. rightmodeler has a dedicated adapter for LiteLLM StandardLoggingPayload logs, and LiteLLM is one of the nine source formats the ingester autodetects.

faq.q: My pipeline uses the Anthropic SDK. How does the swap work?
faq.a: When LiteLLM is the selected replay provider, rightmodeler sets ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to the configured proxy before your pipeline launches. Your code keeps using the native Anthropic SDK.

faq.q: Is re-running my pipeline with a cheaper model safe?
faq.a: The replay runs in a detached git worktree, so writes never touch your real tree; side-effecting tools are mocked from the trace and read-only ones run live. Worktrees isolate files, not runtime; for untrusted or generated code, the run can be wrapped in Docker.

faq.q: Will the end-to-end replay always confirm a swap?
faq.a: No, and that is the point of running it. The E2E pass exists to kill shortlisted swaps that cascade: a candidate that drifts mid-pipeline fails here even if its isolated outputs looked fine. When trajectories diverge or evidence is thin, the audit abstains; a tool that always finds a swap is not measuring anything.
